items.q: What is the difference between a metaphor and a simile?
items.correct: A simile compares using 'like' or 'as'; a metaphor states one thing IS another, making an implicit comparison without those connectives.
items.misconception: Metaphors are short and similes are long.
items.random_wrong: Metaphors are true and similes are false.
items.why: Length is irrelevant. The defining difference is the use of 'like'/'as' in similes — metaphors omit them, making the comparison stronger and more direct.

items.q: What did Romantic poets value in nature?
items.correct: Nature as a source of spiritual insight and emotional truth, accessed through imagination — not picturesque scenery for its own sake.
items.misconception: Romantic poets just liked to write about pretty landscapes.
items.random_wrong: Romantic poets believed nature was full of fairies and elves.
items.why: The Romantic engagement with nature was philosophical — it was a route to truth, the sublime and the self. Reducing it to 'pretty scenery' misses Wordsworth's epistemology entirely.

items.q: What defines epic poetry as a genre?
items.correct: Long narrative poems on heroic subjects, in elevated style, often invoking a muse, in medias res openings, and treating events of national or cosmic significance.
items.misconception: Epic means any poem that is very long.
items.random_wrong: Epic means any story with a battle in it.
items.why: Length alone isn't enough. Epic has specific conventions (invocation, elevated diction, heroic subject) inherited from Homer through Virgil to Milton — students reduce it to scale.
Correct answer: Browning. “Tennyson” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what war was the Light Brigade?
items.why: Correct answer: Crimean. “Boer” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year was Ozymandias published?
items.why: Exact answer: 1818 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
